skill.name: baoyu-danger-gemini-web
skill.description: Generate text and images through a reverse-engineered Gemini Web client using a local TypeScript CLI.
skill.summary: Use Gemini Web for text, image, vision, and session-based generation from a local CLI.
skill.author: jimliu

security_audit.summary: Static findings were noisy for template literals and TypeScript imports, but the credential and network alerts are true positives. The skill opens Chrome with remote debugging, extracts and caches Google session cookies, refreshes them, and sends them to reverse-engineered Gemini Web endpoints. No confirmed malicious exfiltration outside Google services was found, so this is high risk rather than blocked.

high_findings.title: Google Session Cookie Extraction and Caching
high_findings.description: The skill launches Chrome through CDP, reads Google and Gemini cookies, filters them into a cookie map, and writes them to a local cookie file. This is necessary for the advertised reverse-engineered Gemini Web workflow, but it exposes high-value Google session material to local files and any process with access to those paths.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The implementation explicitly calls Network.getCookies for Google URLs and persists the resulting cookie map. This confirms the sensitive credential handling risk, although it is tied to the stated Gemini Web purpose.

high_findings.title: Cookie-Bearing Requests to Reverse-Engineered Gemini Endpoints
high_findings.description: The client sends cached Google cookies and an access token to Gemini Web generation, batch execution, and cookie rotation endpoints. This creates account-session exposure and depends on undocumented Google Web behavior.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code builds Cookie headers from session cookies and posts them to Google Gemini endpoints. There is no evidence of non-Google exfiltration, but the credential-bearing network behavior is confirmed.

high_findings.title: Chrome Process Launch with Remote Debugging
high_findings.description: The skill starts a Chrome or Edge process with a local remote debugging port and an environment-configurable executable path. A malicious or mistaken executable override could run arbitrary local code, and remote debugging expands the local attack surface while the browser is logged in.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The spawn call and remote debugging arguments are directly present. The executable path is constrained by existence checks, but environment and CLI overrides still make this a high-impact local execution path.

medium_findings.title: User-Selected File Upload to Google Services
medium_findings.description: Reference files are read from disk and uploaded to a Google content upload endpoint before they are attached to the Gemini request. This is expected for vision workflows, but it can disclose sensitive local files if an agent passes the wrong path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code verifies that a supplied path is a file, reads its bytes, and uploads it. The upload is user-directed, so this is a meaningful misuse risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Local Session History Persistence
medium_findings.description: Multi-turn sessions save prompts, assistant responses, and Gemini metadata in local JSON files. This can retain sensitive prompt content beyond the active run.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The session save path and message writes are clear. The data is local, so the risk depends on local file access and the sensitivity of prompts.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted Workflow Extension Surface
medium_findings.description: The skill documentation tells agents to load project or home EXTEND.md files before the workflow and says extension content overrides defaults. This creates a prompt-injection surface if those extension files are controlled by an untrusted project or user.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly instructs loading external markdown before the workflow. I found no direct text telling the evaluator to skip security review, but the override mechanism is still risky.

medium_findings.title: Environment and CLI Path Overrides for Sensitive Data
medium_findings.description: Cookie, data, Chrome profile, and Chrome executable locations can be redirected through environment variables or CLI options. This is useful configuration, but it can send credentials to unexpected locations or read from an unintended browser profile.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path override logic is explicit and uses absolute resolution. It is legitimate configuration, but it materially changes where credentials are read or written.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Backtick Alerts Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Many reported backtick findings are TypeScript template literals, string interpolation, or markdown command examples rather than shell execution. The confirmed executable behavior is the Chrome spawn path covered separately.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code and documentation use backticks for strings and examples. Static analysis correctly found risky-looking syntax, but these instances are not direct shell evaluation.

low_findings.title: Import Path Traversal Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The path traversal alerts on ../ imports are standard TypeScript relative imports, not runtime traversal of user-controlled paths. Runtime session IDs are sanitized before being used in filenames.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The import lines are static module references and the session path helper replaces unsafe characters. I did not find evidence of unsanitized traversal in those cited patterns.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Match the Advertised Backend
low_findings.description: The hardcoded network endpoints are Google, Gemini, Google Accounts, Googleusercontent, and Google content upload services. These endpoints are expected for a Gemini Web client, but they should be disclosed because prompts, files, and cookies are sent there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoints are visible and align with the skill purpose. The concern is disclosure and account-session sensitivity, not evidence of a hidden destination.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dangerous Combination: Browser Cookies, Network Requests, and Local Cache
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines browser cookie extraction, credential-bearing network requests, and persistent local cookie storage. This confirms the static analyzer heuristic as a real high-risk pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: All three parts of the heuristic are semantically connected in the authentication flow. The pattern is not hidden, but it is still dangerous for marketplace users.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dangerous Combination: Local File Read and Remote Upload
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill reads local reference files and uploads their bytes to a Google endpoint as part of vision input. This pattern can disclose files when driven by untrusted prompts or automation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The read and upload flow is direct and confirmed. It is user-selected in normal operation, so the risk is misuse and accidental disclosure rather than stealth behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dangerous Pattern: External Instruction Override File
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill asks agents to load EXTEND.md files from project and home paths and allow them to override defaults. This can let untrusted workspace text alter future skill behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation contains an explicit override instruction. I did not find stronger prompt-injection language, so confidence is high enough to warn but not to block.

content.user_title: Generate Gemini Web Images and Text
content.value_statement: Teams need quick image and text generation without building a separate UI. This skill provides a CLI workflow for Gemini Web prompts, images, references, and sessions.
actual_capabilities: Generates text responses from positional prompts, prompt flags, prompt files, or standard input.
Generates images and saves the first returned image to a requested local path.
Accepts local reference images for vision input and variation workflows.
Supports named conversation sessions with saved metadata and message history.
Can refresh Gemini Web authentication cookies through a local Chrome login flow.
Offers plain text output or structured JSON output for downstream tools.
limitations: Uses a reverse-engineered Gemini Web API, not an official Google API.
Requires browser-based Google authentication and cached session cookies.
Can break when Google changes Gemini Web endpoints or response formats.
Uploads reference files and prompts to Google services during generation.

use_cases.title: Create Draft Visuals
use_cases.description: Generate quick concept images from written prompts and save them for review or iteration.

use_cases.title: Produce Assisted Copy
use_cases.description: Send prompt files or short requests to Gemini Web and collect text output from the CLI.

use_cases.title: Automate Visual Variations
use_cases.description: Use reference images and session IDs to run repeatable image variation workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Generate One Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an image of [subject] in [style]. Save it to [file name].

prompt_templates.title: Ask for Text
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Gemini Web to answer this request: [question or writing task].

prompt_templates.title: Use Reference Images
prompt_templates.prompt: Use [reference image paths] as visual references. Generate a new image that changes [specific elements] and keeps [specific elements].

prompt_templates.title: Continue a Session
prompt_templates.prompt: Continue session [session id]. Use the previous context and produce the next output for [task].

output_examples.input: Generate a clean product mockup image for a compact desk lamp.
output_examples.output: Returns a saved image path and optional descriptive text from Gemini Web.

output_examples.input: Explain a technical topic using a prompt file and plain text output.
output_examples.output: Returns a concise text response suitable for review or reuse.

output_examples.input: Use a reference image to create a new visual variation.
output_examples.output: Uploads the reference image, requests a variation, and saves the generated result locally.

best_practices: Use a dedicated Google account because the skill handles browser session cookies.
Review prompt files and reference images before sending them to Gemini Web.
Store generated images and session files in project-specific directories.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill with sensitive Google accounts or regulated data.
Do not pass private source files as reference images or prompt files without review.
Do not allow untrusted EXTEND.md files to modify the workflow.

faq.question: Does this use the official Gemini API?
faq.answer: No. It uses reverse-engineered Gemini Web endpoints and can break without notice.

faq.question: Why does it need Chrome?
faq.answer: The login flow launches Chrome and reads session cookies through the Chrome DevTools Protocol.

faq.question: Where are cookies stored?
faq.answer: Cookies are stored in the skill data directory unless a cookie path override is provided.

faq.question: Can it generate images from reference files?
faq.answer: Yes. It uploads local reference files to Google and uses them in the Gemini request.

faq.question: Can it keep conversation context?
faq.answer: Yes. It stores session metadata and message history in local session files.

faq.question: Is this safe for marketplace publication?
faq.answer: It is high risk. Publication should require strong warnings and review because it handles Google session cookies.
